posts.title: Welcome to the Aspire Blog
posts.excerpt: We're launching the official Aspire Hosting blog — your new home for hosting guides, product updates, infrastructure deep-dives, and the occasional announcement.
posts.content:
We've been meaning to do this for a while.

Hosting is a technical space, and we believe our customers deserve more than just a control panel and a support ticket queue. You deserve context — an explanation of what's running under the hood, honest write-ups about the decisions we make, and practical guides that help you get more out of every server you spin up.

That's what this blog is for.

## What to Expect

We'll be writing about four main topics:

- **Hosting News** — announcements about new locations, hardware upgrades, and service changes
- **Tutorials** — step-by-step guides on deploying servers, configuring bots, optimising game servers, and more
- **Technology** — deep dives into the infrastructure we run and the choices behind it
- **Announcements** — scheduled maintenance windows, policy updates, and anything else you should know

Posts will go up regularly. We're not chasing an algorithm here — just trying to write things worth reading.

## A Note on Quality

Every article on this blog is written by someone who works at Aspire. No filler, no SEO padding, no AI-generated fluff dressed up as expertise. If a post carries a team member's name, they wrote it.

## Where to Start

If you're new to Aspire, a good place to begin is our guide on choosing the right hosting plan for your project — it covers the practical tradeoffs between shared, VPS, and dedicated infrastructure without trying to upsell you at every paragraph.

If you've been a customer for a while, keep an eye on the Technology and Hosting News categories. We'll be sharing more about our infrastructure roadmap there over the coming months.

Welcome aboard.

— The Aspire Team

seo.title: Welcome to the Aspire Blog — Aspire Hosting
seo.description: Introducing the official Aspire Hosting blog — guides, infrastructure deep-dives, product updates, and more.

posts.title: What 99.9% Uptime Actually Means (And Why It Matters)
posts.excerpt: Every hosting provider promises 99.9% uptime. But what does that number actually translate to in downtime per year — and how do you hold a provider accountable to it?
posts.content:
You've seen the number everywhere: **99.9% uptime**. It's on every hosting provider's homepage, usually in large text next to a shield icon. But what does it actually mean in practice?

## The Maths

Uptime is expressed as a percentage of total time in a given period. Here's what the common tiers translate to in real downtime:

| SLA | Downtime per Year | Downtime per Month |
|-----|-------------------|--------------------|
| 99% | ~87.6 hours | ~7.3 hours |
| 99.9% | ~8.7 hours | ~43.8 minutes |
| 99.95% | ~4.4 hours | ~21.9 minutes |
| 99.99% | ~52.6 minutes | ~4.4 minutes |

A 99.9% SLA allows for roughly **43 minutes of downtime per month**. For most workloads, that's acceptable. For payment processing or real-time multiplayer, it might not be.

## What Counts as Downtime?

This is where SLAs get complicated. Most providers define downtime narrowly — often as complete unavailability of the server or service. Partial degradation (slow response times, packet loss, high latency) usually doesn't count.

Read the SLA carefully. Look for:
- How downtime is **measured** (external monitoring vs. internal)
- What's **excluded** (scheduled maintenance, force majeure, user error)
- How you **claim compensation** and what it covers

## Scheduled Maintenance

Nearly every SLA carves out scheduled maintenance windows. During a maintenance window, a provider can take your server down without it counting against their uptime guarantee. Some providers give 24 hours notice; others give a week.

At Aspire, we post maintenance windows at least 72 hours in advance and schedule them during low-traffic hours (typically 1–4 AM UTC). We also post real-time updates on our status page.

## How We Measure It

We use external monitoring — a third-party service pings your server from multiple locations every 60 seconds. If it fails to respond from a majority of locations, we record it as downtime. You can see our historical uptime data on our public status page.

## Bottom Line

99.9% is a reasonable baseline for most hosting use cases. If you need higher reliability, look at providers offering 99.99% SLAs, geo-redundant setups, or automatic failover — and expect to pay accordingly.

The most important question isn't the number itself. It's whether the provider has a track record of hitting it, and what they'll do when they don't.

seo.title: What 99.9% Uptime Actually Means — Aspire Hosting
seo.description: A plain-English breakdown of hosting uptime SLAs, what counts as downtime, and how to hold a provider accountable.

posts.title: How to Choose the Right Hosting Plan for Your Project
posts.excerpt: Shared, VPS, or dedicated — the right answer depends on your traffic, budget, and tolerance for configuration. Here's how to think through it without the upsell.
posts.content:
The most common question we get from new customers is some version of: *"What plan should I get?"*

It's a reasonable question, and the honest answer is: it depends. But "it depends" isn't useful unless we explain what it depends on. So here's the framework we use internally.

## Start With Your Workload

Before looking at any pricing page, answer three questions:

1. **How much traffic do you expect?** A personal project getting 100 visits a day has very different requirements to a community server with 500 concurrent users.
2. **Is your workload CPU-bound or memory-bound?** Game servers eat RAM. Machine learning inference eats CPU. Most web apps are neither — they're network-bound.
3. **How much can go wrong?** If your server going down for an hour costs you money or damages your reputation, you need redundancy. If it's a hobby project, you don't.

## Shared Hosting

**Best for**: Simple websites, portfolios, low-traffic blogs.

Shared hosting puts multiple customers on the same physical server. It's cheap because resources are pooled. The downside is that a noisy neighbour can affect your performance, and you have limited control over the server environment.

If you're running a WordPress blog or a static site, shared hosting is fine. If you're running anything with custom software dependencies, move on.

## VPS Hosting

**Best for**: Most things.

A VPS (Virtual Private Server) gives you a dedicated slice of a physical machine. You get a fixed allocation of RAM and CPU, root access, and the ability to install whatever you want.

The tradeoff is complexity — you're responsible for keeping the operating system patched and the software configured. If you're not comfortable with a Linux terminal, factor in time to learn, or look for managed VPS options.

## Dedicated Servers

**Best for**: High-traffic applications, database servers, anything requiring consistent I/O performance.

With a dedicated server, you're renting the entire physical machine. No neighbours, no contention, predictable performance. It's significantly more expensive than VPS, and most projects don't need it.

Where dedicated makes sense: large game server networks, production databases, applications with sustained high CPU usage, or compliance requirements that prohibit multi-tenancy.

## Game Servers

Game servers are a special case. They're memory-intensive, latency-sensitive, and often need to burst for short periods (when players join). Our game hosting plans are pre-tuned for specific games — the underlying infrastructure is the same VPS hardware, but the software stack is optimised for each title.

Rule of thumb: allocate roughly 500MB RAM per 10 Minecraft players. Discord bots are much lighter — a well-written bot with basic commands will run comfortably on 256–512MB.

## Our Recommendation

If you're unsure, start with a small VPS. It's flexible enough to handle most workloads, and you can scale vertically (more RAM/CPU) or horizontally (more servers) as you grow. Overprovisioning at the start is a common mistake — most projects never hit the limits of a modest VPS.

seo.title: How to Choose the Right Hosting Plan — Aspire Hosting
seo.description: A practical guide to choosing between shared, VPS, and dedicated hosting without the marketing spin.

posts.title: Why Low-Latency Hosting Matters for Online Gaming
posts.excerpt: Ping isn't just a number — it's the difference between a smooth multiplayer experience and one your players rage-quit. Here's what drives latency and how to minimise it.
posts.content:
When a player says a server "feels laggy", they're usually describing one of two things: high latency (ping) or low tick rate. Both are server-side problems, and both are solvable — but they require different fixes.

## What Is Latency?

Latency is the time it takes for a packet to travel from a player's device to the server and back. It's measured in milliseconds (ms). The rough thresholds for gaming are:

- **< 30ms**: Excellent. Players won't notice any delay.
- **30–60ms**: Good. Acceptable for most game types.
- **60–100ms**: Noticeable. Fast-paced games will feel slightly off.
- **> 100ms**: Poor. Competitive players will feel it clearly.

## What Causes Latency?

The biggest driver is **physical distance** between the player and the server. Light travels through fibre at roughly 200,000 km/s — a server in the US will always have higher latency for a player in India than a server in Singapore, regardless of how fast the hardware is.

Other factors:

- **Network routing**: How many hops a packet takes, and the quality of the links between them
- **Server load**: A CPU-bound server introduces processing delay on top of network latency
- **Tick rate**: How often the server processes game state. Higher tick rates (64 vs 128) give more accurate hit detection but increase CPU load

## Choosing a Server Location

The most impactful thing you can do for latency is pick a server location close to your player base. If most of your players are in South Asia, a Mumbai or Singapore server will outperform Frankfurt by 80–120ms.

Our current locations:
- **India (Mumbai)** — lowest latency for South Asian players
- **Singapore** — covers Southeast Asia and Australia
- **Germany (Frankfurt)** — covers Europe
- **USA (East Coast)** — covers North and South America

If your players are spread globally, consider running multiple regional servers and routing players to the nearest one.

## Server-Side Optimisation

Once you've picked the right location, optimise the server itself:

- **Allocate enough RAM**: Swapping to disk adds significant latency
- **Keep the CPU headroom above 70%**: Sustained high CPU usage increases tick processing time
- **Use NVMe storage**: For games that do frequent world saves, disk I/O matters
- **Limit unnecessary plugins**: Each plugin adds overhead to every tick

## Monitoring

Set up latency monitoring from multiple locations, not just your own connection. Tools like our built-in server monitoring, or third-party services, will alert you if players in a specific region are experiencing degraded performance — before they open a support ticket.

Good latency is mostly about geography and resource allocation. Get those right, and most other issues are marginal.

seo.title: Why Low-Latency Hosting Matters for Online Gaming — Aspire Hosting
seo.description: A technical guide to understanding and reducing server latency for game hosting — location selection, tick rate, and optimisation.
